factual-accuracy.requirement: States HCI timing guidelines approximately 0.1s instantaneous, 1s preserves thought, beyond 10s loses attention
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states the classic HCI timing thresholds as 0.1 seconds (instantaneous), 1.0 seconds (flow of thought uninterrupted), and 10 seconds (attention limit), including this summary in both the executive summary and Section 1.1.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States inline suggestions should target 150-300ms first render to preserve flow state
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives latency targets like “Single-Line Autocomplete (Requires <200ms for Flow)” and “Inline autocomplete … <200ms,” but it never states that inline suggestions should target a 150–300ms first render window. The specific 150–300ms threshold and the framing as “first render to preserve flow state” are missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States multi-line suggestions acceptable at 300-1000ms with boundaries and visual affordances
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states multi-line predictions are acceptable in the 200–500ms range and later gives a 200–600ms target, and it also describes boundaries (workflow boundaries like post-commit/file save) plus visual affordances (presence indicators/interaction scopes, feedback indicators).

factual-accuracy.requirement: States comment-to-code generation tolerable at 0.5-2 seconds with streaming or progress indicators
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states “Comment-to-Code Generation (1-3s Acceptable)” and later gives a target of “<2500ms” for comments→code, and notes that this latency is tolerable when paired with visual feedback (i.e., progress/feedback indicators).

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends streaming, progress indicators, or on-demand mode when generation exceeds timing budgets
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends using on-demand invocation in multiple places (e.g., “On-demand only” during debugging and “More on-demand invocation” for seniors) and explicitly notes that higher latencies “require feedback indicator,” implying progress indicators when timing budgets are exceeded.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends on-demand or delayed triggers for debugging and proactive acceptable for feature development
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly recommends on-demand invocation for debugging (“Debugging… On-demand only”) and states proactive suggestions are acceptable for feature development at workflow boundaries (“New feature development… Proactive at boundaries, on-demand during flow”). It also frames these as evidence-based timing guidelines.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States debugging has shorter edit-run cycles (approximately 1 minute) than feature development (approximately 3 minutes)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses interruption costs and gives latency budgets by task type, but it never states specific edit-run cycle durations for debugging (~1 minute) versus feature development (~3 minutes). No explicit comparison of those approximate cycle times appears anywhere in the text.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States less-experienced developers show higher AI suggestion acceptance rates than 10+ year developers
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses juniors having higher trust ("78% trust... vs 39% for seniors") and various productivity/acceptance metrics, but it does not state that less-experienced developers have higher AI suggestion acceptance rates than developers with 10+ years experience. No explicit acceptance-rate comparison by experience level (2–5 vs 10+) is provided.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Compares GitHub Copilot inline suggestions, Tabnine multi-line predictions, and CodeWhisperer comment-to-code
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly compares GitHub Copilot’s inline autocomplete latency/behavior, Tabnine’s multi-line (local/cloud) prediction approach, and Amazon CodeWhisperer/Amazon Q’s comment-to-code generation modality, including a cross-vendor table contrasting these suggestion types.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Reports acceptance rates 25-35% in production deployment
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response reports production deployment acceptance rates within the 25–35% range, including GitHub Copilot at 33% (ZoomInfo enterprise study) and a general Copilot range of 27–33%. These figures are presented as real-world/enterprise deployment metrics rather than lab-only results.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Reports Copilot 10-15% acceptance gain OR CodeWhisperer 50-60% faster OR JetBrains 40-50% acceptance increase
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response reports a JetBrains metric that Mellum achieves acceptance rates around 30–44% and also mentions Copilot’s “12% higher acceptance rate,” but it does not state Copilot having a 10–15% acceptance gain, CodeWhisperer being 50–60% faster, or JetBrains having a 40–50% acceptance increase. None of the specified numeric ranges/claims appear as written.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies trade-off between acceptance-rate optimization and code quality or retention metrics
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly discusses a trade-off where proactive/acceptance-rate-focused assistance increases efficiency but can disrupt workflow and harm understanding/ownership, and it contrasts acceptance/retention metrics with downstream costs like debugging, review time, and code comprehension/quality.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Synthesizes conditional decision framework specifying when proactive versus on-demand presentation
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response provides an explicit proactive vs. on-demand decision framework, including sections on “Suggestion Timing: Proactive vs. On-Demand,” with rules tied to developer state (acceleration vs exploration), task type (debugging vs feature work), and workflow boundaries, plus a latency-budget table mapping scenarios to recommended timing.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Extracts vendor-specific deployment techniques with measured outcomes (e.g., filter +50% acceptance)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response includes vendor-specific deployment techniques (e.g., Copilot proxy/HTTP2 routing, Tabnine hybrid local+cloud with CatBoost filter, JetBrains FLCC local inference, Bedrock latency-optimized inference) and pairs them with measured outcomes such as “12% higher acceptance rate,” “20% more accepted characters,” “1.3x–1.5x usage increase,” and TTFT/latency reductions (e.g., -42% to -51%).

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides experience-level differentiated strategies with specific recommendations per cohort
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response differentiates strategies for junior (2–5 years) versus senior (10+ years) developers, including distinct guidance on proactive vs on-demand timing, explanation depth, confidence indicators, and forcing functions. It provides specific cohort-targeted recommendations in Sections 7.2 and 7.3.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Specifies escalation paths when timing budgets exceeded (streaming, progress indicators, on-demand)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response provides explicit latency budgets by scenario and modality (e.g., inline <200ms, proactive boundary <500ms, on-demand 1–3s) and notes UI handling when latency is higher (e.g., “requires visual feedback indicator” for 1–2s cloud). It also distinguishes proactive vs on-demand timing contexts, which functions as an escalation path when timing budgets are exceeded.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Distinguishes appropriate trust calibration from blind acceptance with UI implementation patterns
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly contrasts calibrated trust with overreliance (e.g., “avoid blind acceptance,” “overtrust,” “automation bias”) and ties this to concrete UI patterns such as confidence scores, progressive disclosure, cognitive forcing functions (wait/on-demand/human-goes-first), and dashboards/quality indicators to support appropriate trust calibration.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Acknowledges evidence gaps and recommends instrumentation for uncertain areas
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly identifies multiple evidence gaps (e.g., lack of controlled latency comparisons, experience-level latency tolerance data, and production A/B tests for explanations) and recommends instrumentation/metrics to address uncertainty (e.g., tracking P50/P90/P99 latency, flow disruption frequency, perception-actuality gap, churn, and retention).

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Recommends proactive suggestions as universally beneficial without acknowledging flow state disruption or interruption cost research
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly emphasizes interruption costs and flow disruption (e.g., resumption costs, 10–45 minute recovery) and explicitly recommends avoiding proactive suggestions mid-task, especially during debugging. It frames proactivity as context-dependent (workflow boundaries vs. active coding) rather than universally beneficial.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses precise timing terminology (latency thresholds, render time, flow state) with explicit millisecond or second units
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly uses precise timing terminology (e.g., TTFT, P50/P90/P99 latency, flow-preserving/flow-disruptive regimes) and provides explicit units in milliseconds and seconds (e.g., 15–80ms, 200ms, 0.7s, 1–2 seconds, 42.8s).

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes findings by tool (Copilot, Tabnine, CodeWhisperer) and cross-cuts by experience levels and task types
presentation-quality.explanation: The response includes multiple cross-vendor comparison sections and tables that explicitly break out GitHub Copilot, Tabnine, and Amazon Q/CodeWhisperer, and it repeatedly cross-cuts results by experience level (juniors vs seniors) and by task type (debugging/fix vs new feature/documentation). It also ties timing/latency and proactive vs on-demand guidance to those experience/task distinctions.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains objective research synthesis tone without marketing language or anecdotal personal experience
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses a formal, research-synthesis style with structured sections, quantitative claims, and citations. It does not include anecdotal personal experience or first-person storytelling. While it contains some evaluative phrasing (e.g., “catastrophic”), it does not use promotional/marketing language.

presentation-quality.requirement: Concludes key sections with specific design recommendations derived from the research evidence
presentation-quality.explanation: The response ends with a dedicated “Practical Recommendations” section that gives concrete design targets (e.g., latency thresholds by suggestion type, proactive vs on-demand timing rules, and confidence-score/explanation guidance) explicitly tied back to cited research findings throughout the report.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents comparisons as prose paragraphs rather than using tables, parallel sections, or labeled side-by-side format
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses numerous tables and labeled sections (e.g., multiple markdown tables for latency, acceptance rates, task comparisons) rather than presenting comparisons purely as prose paragraphs.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites academic research on programmer interruption costs or AI code completion from arXiv, ACM, IEEE, or ResearchGate
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites multiple academic sources on interruption costs and AI code completion from arXiv and ACM/CHI venues, including arXiv papers (e.g., 2405.08704, 2302.06590, 2601.10253) and an ACM FAccT 2024 DOI, plus classic interruption studies (Monk et al., Parnin & Rugaber, Mark et al.).

citation-quality.requirement: References JetBrains AI assistant deployment metrics or timing research from blog.jetbrains.com or official JetBrains publications
citation-quality.explanation: The response includes multiple JetBrains official publications from blog.jetbrains.com (e.g., JetBrains AI Assistant survey, Mellum training post, Next Edit Suggestions GA post) and cites JetBrains deployment metrics such as latency (<200ms, 50–75ms), acceptance rates, and usage increases.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites GitHub Copilot or Microsoft productivity studies from github.blog, microsoft.com/research, or ACM publications
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites GitHub Copilot research from GitHub Blog (e.g., source [8] on github.blog) and includes an ACM publication from Microsoft Research (FAccT 2024 paper, source [52] via dl.acm.org). These satisfy the requirement to cite GitHub Copilot or Microsoft productivity studies from the specified domains.

citation-quality.requirement: References established HCI timing thresholds (Nielsen, speedcurve.com, or equivalent authoritative HCI sources)
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly cites authoritative HCI timing thresholds, including Jakob Nielsen’s 0.1s/1s/10s limits and Miller’s response-time thresholds, and uses these as the basis for latency regime mapping.

citation-quality.requirement: Includes research or metrics from at least 2 of AWS CodeWhisperer, Google Research, Tabnine, or other code completion vendors
citation-quality.explanation: The response includes vendor-specific metrics for Tabnine (e.g., 15–80ms TTFT, acceptance rates) and AWS CodeWhisperer/Amazon Q Developer (e.g., Bedrock latency-optimized TTFT reductions and inline completion latency), satisfying the requirement for at least two listed vendors.

citation-quality.requirement: Grounds timing recommendations in cited research rather than personal anecdotes or team experiences
citation-quality.explanation: The response consistently grounds timing and latency recommendations in cited HCI and interruption research (e.g., Miller/Nielsen thresholds, Monk/Parnin interruption costs) and vendor/academic studies, with extensive source links. It does not rely on personal anecdotes or team experience as justification for timing guidance.

citation-quality.requirement: Grounds timing recommendations solely in personal blogs, forums, or social media without citing academic papers or official vendor research
citation-quality.explanation: The response grounds its timing and latency recommendations in a mix of academic papers (e.g., Monk et al., Parnin & Rugaber, Card/Moran/Newell), and official vendor research/blogs (GitHub, AWS, JetBrains), not solely in blogs/forums/social media. While it includes some blog/LinkedIn/Medium sources, these are not the exclusive basis for timing guidance.
